Out of curiosity, does any tool use `CMAKE_ARGS` other than conda-forge builds? What concerns me a little here is the possibility that scikit-build is being developed for compatibility with conda-forge rather than the other way around. I think it's fine for scikit-build (which aims to be a reasonably drop-in replacement for setuptools) but I want to make sure that same mindset doesn't percolate to scikit-build-core. With modern PEP 517 builders, which could all be developed completely independently of one another, I think the responsibility should be on whatever invokes a build tool (e.g. conda-forge) to set up arguments in a way that is compatible with any builder that it wants to support.

        try:
            srcs = eval(
                src_txt.replace('mediaServer.vod', 'vod').replace(',\n',', '),
                {'vod': media_server.get('vod', 'ms02.w24.at'), 'src': 'src', 'type': 'type'})
            # expect a non-empty list 
            srcs[0]    
        except Exception as e:
            raise ExtractorError('Unable to extract media links', cause=e)
        formats = []
        for src in srcs:
            fmt_url = url_or_none(src.get('src'))
            if not fmt_url:
                continue
            ext = mimetype2ext(src.get('type')) or determine_ext(fmt_url)
            if ext == 'm3u8':
                formats.extend(self._extract_m3u8_formats(
                    fmt_url, video_id, 'mp4',
                    entry_protocol='m3u8_native', m3u8_id='hls', fatal=False))
            else:
                formats.append({
                    'url': fmt_url,
                    'ext': ext,
                })
```

```suggestion
import imageio.v3 as iio
```

I'd generally recommend importing the version-major specific namespace (we also have `imageio.v2`) when using ImageIO. 

Both APIs will continue to exist going forward, but this way communicates to a fellow maintainer if they should use `v2.volread` or `v3.imread(..., index=...)` when modifying the file. It also has the nice side-effect of versioning scripts, because the expected version major is explicitly written into the import statement: "I can't remember which versions I used for this 3 years ago, but it says `import imageio.v3`, so I guess we could start with trying ImageIO v3.X", and it will just work. It also makes code more robust to future breaking changes to the API (in ImageIO v4+), which will exist in `import imageio.v4` should the need ever arise.

*Edit:* Also `iio.imread` looks way cooler than `imageio.imread` 😆

`MKLDNN_POST_QUANTIZE` is for optimizing quantized model. it should be used in quantization flow only, by inserting `qsym = qsym.get_backend_symbol('MKLDNN_POST_QUANTIZE')` explicitly.

Yup, I am on board with doing this in future work. Having an immutable version of the trie has been on my wishlist for a long time. I listed it as a potential breaking change to make the default trie immutable: https://github.com/ethereum/py-trie/issues/85

> The first one might be better though:
> 
> ```
> # local mutation
> with trie.at_root(root_hash):
>     trie.get(key)
> ```
> If it mutates the old trie then there's no chance for users to accidentally concurrently modify a copy.

Here's my problem with that API:

```py
class TrieUser:
    def __init__(self, trie):
        self.trie = trie

    async def look_up_old_val(self, key, root):
        with trie.at_root(root):

            # this checks if nodes are missing from the trie
            while busted(trie):
                await fix_up_trie(trie)  # this is adding nodes to the trie

            # this is returning the key at whatever root hash `trie` has
            return trie[key]

    def trie_updates(self, key, val):
        # Updates might come in while awaiting above.
        # Any reference to trie now has the state root from within that context
        # even though it never invoked the context.
        # Further, the trie updates `root_hash` after setting the key & val,
        # so the returned value above is from a different root
        trie[key] = val
```

partition_key is a part of the EventDataBatch. Once an EventDataBatch is built, the partition_key can't be changed for two reasons:
1. The EventDataBatch size will be changed if partition_key is changed.
2. We serialize event(with partition_key) into EventDataBatch when the event is added to EventDataBatch. It's hard to change it once serialized.

To make it easy for users to understand, when the input is an EventDataBatch, we'll validate that partition_key and partition_id must be None. We can raise a TypeError.

There's no 'standard' with TS that I'm aware of (Was there ever one for web dev tools 🙃)
Example is [`@types/node`](https://github.com/DefinitelyTyped/DefinitelyTyped/tree/master/types/node/v8) or `octokit/rest.js`.
Node has the official docs but typescript specific bindings for something like `Buffer` for example are documented. Octokit on the other hand has most of their docs and types as part of their `octokit/types.ts` repo which wrap around some other libraries and JS modules.

```
2943  RAN: /usr/bin/python2 -m Cython.Build.Cythonize ./jnius/jnius.pyx
2944
2945  STDOUT:
2946/usr/bin/python2: No module named Cython.Build
```
So that's properly weird and annoying. Basically it means if we want to support the "Python2 basic" apk build in the CI, we do need the system cython on python2. While only having the venv cython3. That would look kinda inconsistent in the Dockerfile, but with some comments that should make it.
So basically we want to have:
```
RUN pip2 install --upgrade Cython==0.28.6
```
And also in the venv:
```
&& pip3 install --upgrade Cython==0.28.6
```
By the way, let's take the opportunity to make them consistent.

@djhoese, please do not get me wrong, I am not against using the patterns, I just do not like that it constraints the data storage architecture. The current implementation only knows file systems, but imagine a situation where you are dealing with an object storage, which is designed to quickly provide the relevant metadata and a way to access the data itself. And even if you store the files on a file system, you might have a database containing the relevant metadata. In this case the pattern matching might be overhead, because it is certainly faster to query the database to get the metadata instead of decomposing a filename. For such use cases, it might be great to have an alternative way.
For a comprehensive implementation, it would be good to separate the extraction of required metadata by pattern matching from the subsequent sorting, grouping, ... operations, which I hope and guess that it can be done without adding too much overhead. In case the metadata is already present, it should be fine to skip this step.

> Having the user provide the information while possible, is not something non-programmer users can do.

I know that `satpy` aims at being a convenience product and I personally also appreciate it, but adding such an alternative metadata interface would not break any existing workflow. It only provides an interface for advanced use cases. 

I hope to find some time these days to present an implementation proposal.

Yes, the typing of `flatten` and `unflatten` in the PR prior to this review comment was problematic in that it declared the mixed-type-list idiom, meaning it was typically necessary to cast whatever went into or out of the methods. The reason I had chosen to declare the types like this was that I found it important to maintain the contained type within an input list as the output type (for `flatten`) / to maintain the input type as the contained type within the output list (for `unflatten`), i.e. to make it clear that if you put say an `Ints3d` in, you will always get a `List[Ints3d]` out.

I think I've now found a solution that offers the best of both worlds. which is essentially to use the type signatures @svlandeg suggested but additionally to use `@overload` signature declarations to express the bindings between specific array types and the corresponding array-containing list types. I have only added these for those types that seem to be used frequently: `Floats2d`; `Ints1d` and `Array2d`. If people think it sensible I could add more, but remembering that people can always use the `ListXd` / `ArrayXd` solution, casting if necessary, this did not seem necessary.

I am very confident now that this is an appropriate solution because far from this causing new `mypy` errors it rather allowed the removal of a slew of `cast`s.

This function below is a minimal change to correct the ROM table address on AP#0.

```py
    def create_init_sequence(self):
        # Insert init task to correct the ROM table base address value that incorrectly has the
        # P (preset) bit 0 cleared in hardware.

        def fixup_rom_base():
            self.aps[0].rom_addr = 0xE00FF00
            self.aps[0].has_rom_table = True
        
        seq = super().create_init_sequence()
        seq.wrap_task('discovery',
            lambda seq: seq.insert_after('create_aps', ('fixup_rom_base', fixup_rom_base))
            )
        return seq
```

The error is supposed to indicate an issue where it's unclear which class the attribute would come from:
```
>>> class A:            
...   __slots__ = ("a",)
... 
>>> class B:            
...   __slots__ = ("a",)
... 
>>> class C(A, B):
...  __slots__ = ()
... 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: multiple bases have instance lay-out conflict
```

It's interesting that the same error occurs with `int`. This doesn't support non-empty slots due to being variable length, which would normally give an error like:
```
>>> class C(int):
...  __slots__ = ("c",)
... 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: nonempty __slots__ not supported for subtype of 'int'
```

Maybe there is some other restriction with C classes that I couldn't find documented.

I got the impression that "skipping over as if it doesn't exist" might have been intended in `forward()` like what is done in `viterbi_decode`. However, I don't think this is actually what the code does, as if there are multiple mask False values in a row, alpha will go to zero, not retain the previous token. This means if we had a mask like [[0, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 1]] we would "forget" about the likelihood of the first span.

Additionally it seems like https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/4b467ec2882c49c9162d37f40fb55e08d7f5fee0/allennlp/modules/conditional_random_field.py#L298-L301
will potentially treat the middle of the sequence as connecting to END if there are False values in the middle of the mask. In order to be able to distinguish between actual ends, and masks that end early, I think the user would need to pass in sequence lengths into `forward()`. 

Similarly masking for the START is not being handled.

Given the loss values are not quite correct, it seems like masks which do not represent pads at the end should be disallowed. 9b5c9c0 explains this and adds a check for unsupported masks.

If this seems ok, I can make an issue explaining how the calculation might be wrong, and add a commit to this PR linking to it in the docstring / error message. Having False values in places besides the end of the mask seems like a rather niche/low-priority use case for most models (I might be missing something though).

I feel a little guilty committing something that introduces a new exception, so I could also change this to raise a warning explaining the loss might not be correct for non-pad masks, rather than exception, if we think there are people who might depend the current (if slightly incorrect) behavior.

Thanks for the question. In my opinion, there are a few advantages of using a dict. Example:

```
#native definition
tableNativeNameToCode = {
	'NX_KEYTYPE_SOUND_UP': 0,
	'NX_KEYTYPE_SOUND_DOWN': 1,
	'NX_KEYTYPE_BRIGHTNESS_UP': 2,
	'NX_KEYTYPE_BRIGHTNESS_DOWN': 3
}

#native names to pynput naming, 1:1 visual aid
tableNativeToPynput = {
	'NX_KEYTYPE_SOUND_UP': 'media_volume_up',
	'NX_KEYTYPE_SOUND_DOWN': 'media_volume_down',
	'NX_KEYTYPE_BRIGHTNESS_UP': 'media_brightness_up',
	'NX_KEYTYPE_BRIGHTNESS_DOWN': 'media_brightness_down'
}

#useful for key controller
tablePynputToCode = dict( zip( tableNativeToPynput.values(), tableNativeNameToCode.values() ) )

#useful for key listener
tableCodeToPynput = dict( zip( tableNativeNameToCode.values(), tableNativeToPynput.values() ) )
```

This is cleaner (not only visually but can have the declarations all together - header-like), more flexible (easier to add, remove, edit, merge) and therefore easier to maintain (can track the number of keys for testing too) 

It also allows neat usages besides the usual tablePynputToCode['media_volume_up']:

```
tablePynputToCode.get('unknown_key',None) #have a default value in case key is not found
#or
def pynputToCode( x ):
    return tablePynputToCode.get(x,None)
```

And since it is a dict, it can be iterated as well to see the keys that are supported, etc

> It's true that implementing traits like this requires a dynamic check. Asking because if we're happy with "loose" trait concretization, then `(impl-trait)` feels more like a hint for our _tooling_ to check that all trait methods are implemented.

        sampled_indices = torch.multinomial(
            normalized_top_k_probs, per_node_beam_size, replacement=self.replacement
        )

        # Convert `sampled_indices` back to indices in the original `log_probs` tensor.
        # shape: (batch_size, per_node_beam_size)
        indices = top_k_indices.gather(-1, sampled_indices)

        return log_probs.gather(1, indices), indices, state
```

```suggestion
        ## Common bug to avoid in your implementation
        
        In the previous code example, note that we called a module-scoped
        function, `_your_function_that_gets_neighbors_for_a_given_token`,
        instead of one defined in the scope of `project_neighbors`.  Because
        Python evaluates references to variables in outer scopes at the time a
        function or generator is invoked--not at the time it's defined--, it's
        very easy to introduce subtle race conditions when defining generator
        factories in a nested scope.
        
        Because generators may be evaluated in arbitrary order, these bugs can
        appear only intermittently and can be very difficult to troubleshoot.
        Always defining generator factories in the module scope is one
        reliable way to avoid this problem.
        
        In this example code, we use a for-loop to yield several generators from
        a generator factory.  Notice that we don't pass any arguments to the
        generator factory--the value its generators yield come from its
        enclosing scope.
        
        >>> def yield_generators():
        ...     for target in range(1, 4):
        ...         def _generator_factory():
        ...             while True:
        ...                 # refers to `target` in the enclosing scope
        ...                 yield target
        ...         yield _generator_factory()
        ...
        >>> gens = yield_generators()
        >>> one = next(gens)
        >>> next(one)
        1
        >>> two = next(gens)
        >>> next(two)
        2
        >>> next(one)  # We expect 1, but get 2
        2
        >>> three = next(gens)
        >>> next(three)
        3
        >>> next(two)  # We expect 2, but get 3
        3
        >>> next(one)  # We expect 1, got 2, and now get 3
        3
        
        Although we have three distinct generators, they're all yielding the
        same `target` from `yield_generator`'s scope, which is also the same
        `target` that the for-loop advances with each iteration.
        
        If we define `_generator_factory` in the scope of the module, then we
        can't refer inadvertently to shared state in an enclosing scope, which
        saves us from this bug.
        
        >>> def _generator_factory(target):
        ...     while True:
        ...         # refers to the argument `target`, which exists
        ...         # only in the local scope
        ...         yield target
        ...
        >>> def yield_generators():
        ...     for target in range(1, 4):
        ...         yield _generator_factory(target)
        ...
        >>> gens = yield_generators()
        >>> one = next(gens)
        >>> next(one)
        1
        >>> two = next(gens)
        >>> next(two)
        2
        >>> next(one)
        1
        >>> three = next(gens)
        >>> next(three)
        3
        >>> next(two)
        2
        >>> next(one)
        1
```

This will clean out any rererences from the `refs/west` ref space, unless revision is a non-SHA and identical to current revision in the manifest file.

As all references west are removed, it means that all SHAs not referenced from elsewhere (as example a tag or local branch) will be a loose object.
Loose objects are per default pruned after two weeks.
https://git-scm.com/docs/git-gc 
I believe, that this approach should be safe, as two weeks is most likely long enough time for objects not referenced elsewhere.

But if desired, it could be considered to make an entry in reflog.
The disadvantage of that, is that it starts making a lot of `west` caused entries, which might be annoying for some users (personally I prefer that reflog is populated mainly to `git` operations I have made).

So I propose to wait for user feedback before populating the reflog.

The following code presents how the reflog could be populated.
Reflog default expires after 90 days.

It does the same thing that `derive(Eq)` would do, since `Eq` itself has
no methods (it’s a marker interface). But you’re only permitted to
`derive(Eq)` on types all of whose fields are also `Eq`. In this case,
we have a field of type `f64`, which is _not_ `Eq` (because of NaN), so
we cannot use `derive(Eq)`:

```diff
$ git diff -U1
diff --git a/tensorboard/data/server/types.rs b/tensorboard/data/server/types.rs
index 59a764504..755329912 100644
--- a/tensorboard/data/server/types.rs
+++ b/tensorboard/data/server/types.rs
@@ -26,3 +26,3 @@ pub struct Step(pub i64);
 /// Wall times represent floating-point seconds since Unix epoch. They must be finite and non-NaN.
-#[derive(Debug, PartialEq, PartialOrd, Copy, Clone)]
+#[derive(Debug, PartialEq, PartialOrd, Copy, Clone, Eq)]
 pub struct WallTime(f64);
@@ -51,3 +51,2 @@ impl Ord for WallTime {
 }
-impl Eq for WallTime {}
 
```

```
$ cargo check
error[E0277]: the trait bound `f64: std::cmp::Eq` is not satisfied
   --> types.rs:28:21
    |
28  | pub struct WallTime(f64);
    |                     ^^^ the trait `std::cmp::Eq` is not implemented for `f64`
    | 
```

Instead, `impl Eq for WallTime {}` is our explicit way of declaring that
our type really does have a total equivalence relation, even though the
types of its fields do not.

function body, because:

  - default argument values are evaluated at function definition time,

  - using `None` as a default argument value in general is a [common
    Python pattern][none] enabling callers to specify “use default”
    without having to duplicate the actual value of the default

(Likewise below.)

[style]: https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html#2124-decision
[none]: https://unhashable.com/always-use-none-for-default-args-in-python/

I think this can be done better. We have the following cases to support:

1. "Trying out"/sandbox: download the container, run it, everything works, equivalent of what's running at sandbox.tinode.co: init db, load sample data, run tinode. This is probably the most common use case.
2. First production run: init db without loading sample data, run tinode.
3. Run container after stopping: just run tinode.
4. Upgrade DB: run init-db --upgrade=true, run tinode.
5. Reset DB: with or without loading sample data.

We can check if the container is started for the first time by checking for a local "cookie" file. For case 1, 2, 3 If the file is missing, run init-db then write the file. Otherwise just run tinode (3).

There is a question if we should load sample data by default. Either way is OK, but we should support three cases:
a. Load stock sample data
b. Load custom sample data
c. Do not load sample data at all

I think the default should be a. b & c can be controlled by supplying a path to sample data, including an empty path. The empty path is currently not possible. 

 The most common case should be the simplest.
 
Cases 4 & 5 can only be triggered with an explicit ENV var, so the way it's done now is good.

Fix ` AttributeError: <MagicMock id='2694374960976'> does not have the attribute '__version__'` error.
In 3.11, mock can only mock selected magic method, `__version__` is not in mockable.
> The only exceptions are magic methods and attributes (those that have leading and trailing double underscores). Mock doesn’t create these but instead raises an [AttributeError](https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#AttributeError). This is because the interpreter will often implicitly request these methods, and gets very confused to get a new Mock object when it expects a magic method. If you need magic method support see [magic methods](https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.mock.html#magic-methods).

Ref: https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.mock.html#id3

@EskoDijk Thank you for looking into the change at such a level of detail! This ensures that we don't overlook anything and that everyone understands what this implies. Let me try to answer your questions as precisely as I can.

> Is it intentional that kRadioHeaderShrDuration is removed here -- so that the point of reference for nextTxWindow becomes "end of last symbol of SFD" ?

Moving to a common standard based definition for TX and RX timestamps was the central motivation for this PR as those definitions were inconsistent in OT AFAICS (see detailed rationale of my choice of timestamp definition below).

> Because this changes the calculation, it also means that code below in sub_mac.cpp becomes invalid:

I don't think so. The change was introduced asymmetrically as the TX side was already based on the SFD definition (at least in Zepyhr's platform adaptation layer). I may have done it wrong or my memory may fail me - but that was the intention. @edmont You're probably in a much better place to explain the details on the OT side or see the errors in my implementation or argument.

> Maybe the change on the txWindow calculations can be done in a separate PR? As it modifies the calculation and breaks any platforms that use a non-time-scheduled Tx platform.

All the documentation is just preparative to introduce the concepts needed to define timestamps with the precision required by the IEEE 802.15.4 standard itself. The documentation describes and explains the implementation change and can therefore not be separated from it IMHO. I just did two commits to ensure that the implementation change is not "lost in the noise". Of course I may be wrong or the documentation may not reflect the implementation - so I hope such errors will be unveiled in this review.

This PR proposes a common definition for TX and RX timestamps for the following reasons:
* Such an inconsistency is difficult to reason about and easy to overlook. My intention is to reduce cognitive load and improve maintainability (better long term TCO at the price of short term refactoring effort).
* There is also a technical argument that might not (yet) be relevant to OpenThread but this can easily change in the future (as the Thread standard may not ignore highly relevant recent PHYs like UWB or SUN FSK forever). The IEEE 802.15.4 standard defines a "start of frame delimiter" that it guarantees to be consistently defined across all current and future PHYs such that MAC protocols can be based on that definition regardless of PHY details. SFD is defined as the earliest point in time at which the existence of a frame on the medium can be predicted with sufficiently high probablility and precision (including ranging requirements) for a given RF demodulation/correlation/filter technology. The SHR duration depends very much on the PHY and can therefore not be consistently used on MAC level. AFAIK this is the reason why most frame timings in the standard are relative to the "start of the first symbol after the SFD" or "end of SFD". If even more precision is required (e.g. in ranging) then the timestamp reference plane is defined to be "the local antenna" and called "RMARKER" (but it's the same basic concept). The fact that CSL also introduces timings relative to the start of the MAC header is probably to ease the implementation of the corresponding IE parsers and as you can see from my formula is not a problem as long as the PHY specific offsets cancel. Such a definition is not adequate for general timestamps independent of the IEEE 802.15.4 sub-protocol, though, and should therefore not be used outside CSL standard for general RX/TX timestamps.

I agree that it is unfortunate to fix such an inconsistency after it has been implemented by all platforms. But OTOH the longer it lives the harder it will be to fix. So IMO it's still cheaper to fix it now than in two years.

IMO the change is less than what you assume, though. It's just that the length of the SHR (which is just a simple well-defined us-precision constant for the given PHY) may have to be added in platform implementations that choose to use different timestamp definitions internally. In Zephyr we simply migrated our whole stack down to the driver level to a standard definition - but that's our choice of course. I think it's obvious that this is the right thing to do for Zephyr (which already supports all kinds of PHYs and IEEE 802.15.4 standard protocols and expects to do some work in ranging soon).

That said, I'm just offering my help here. Zephyr's decision to use standard timestamps can easily be isolated from the OT API by transforming timestamps internally to Zephyr's OT platform adaptation layer. So of course the OT community may legitimately decide to use different timestamp definition for whatever theoretical or practical reasons (which would be a valid result of the review!).

I don't think `<>()` are safe when they are not quoted. (Some users new to bash may forget to do that.) `<>` can be parsed as `stdin` `stdout` redirection, and `()` can be parsed as [subshell execution](https://www.gnu.org/software/bash/manual/html_node/Command-Grouping.html):

```
$ echo a>b
# Output is redirected

$ echo (ab)
-bash: syntax error near unexpected token `ab'
```

`%` is also not safe in a Windows Command Prompt: https://ss64.com/nt/syntax-percent.html

We will sync internally with the security team first and try to find out the best solution. 

Possible solutions are:

1. The first character must be a letter or number, then the rest characters can only use punctuation in `-_.~`
2. Show a warning when generating the password, something like "when using the password, it must be quoted to avoid shell interpretation"

I wonder if the problem is mostly coming at the corners.  The corners of pixel (0,0) move basically directly inward.  So they are pretty accurate from the (0,0) pixel's own coordinates for that point, as the angle of the 45 degree direction is still pointing exactly at the corner.
For the upper right corner, the (1,1) pixel's lower left direction probably still has a good estimate for this point.
But the (0,1) and (1,0) pixels will not be aiming at the right place for their upper left and lower right directions (respectively), so they will hit different points on the curve that aren't actually at the corner point.  Averaging these two with the two accurate ones will bias the corner position outward.
I'm thinking maybe the right procedure is not to average these values, but rather to trust the side that is closer to the center pixel.  So the central pixel gets all of its own values from the original file that Craig verified.  Then the 4 orthogonal pixels use the central pixel's values for the sides they share with it, but their own values for the rest.  And so forth.  Always trusting the values for the pixel with the smaller |i| or |j| as appropriate.  I think this might give more accurate pixel areas.  Especially (0,0) whose area should be literally the same.

- Setting the new flags to `old_config | gc.DEBUG_SAVEALL` so that any other flags set for debugging aren't wiped.
- Doing the initial `gc.collect()` before setting the flag, so that cyclic but collectable garbage that existed before the test can be freed rather than being stuck forever in `gc.garbage`.

`.eop()` == "end of path".  It will return `true` only if the cursor has walked past the last byte of the path.  It will return `false` if there are still bytes to walk in the path.

`.eonp()` == "end of node path".  It will return `true` only if the cursor has walked past the last byte of the compressed path segment within the currently visited node.  It will return `false` if the path being walked and the node's compressed path diverge from one another.

For example, consider the path `00 01 02 03 04 05 06 07 08 09` (paths in the code are 32 bytes, but this will work for our purposes).  Suppose that there's a trie composed of a `Node256` with a child `Node4`, which in turn has a `Leaf`, with the following compressed path segments:

```
Node256[path=00010203] - (04)Node4[path=050607] - (08)Leaf[path=09]=xxxxxxxx
```

So, the `Node256` has the compressed path segment `00 01 02 03`, the `Node4` has the compressed path segment `05 06 07`, and the `Leaf` has the compressed path segment `09`.

Now, suppose we create a `TrieCursor` on the path `00 01 02 03 04 05 ff 07 08 09`.  On the first call to `.walk()`, the cursor will return the pointer for character `04`, and `.eonp()` will be `true` and `.eop()` will be `false`.  This is because the call to `.walk()` will advance the cursor through the `Node256`'s compressed path segment, and reach child `04`.  Since the slot for child `04` is filled, `.walk()` returns a pointer (and not `None`).

On the second call to `.walk()`, the cursor will visit the `Node4` and return `None` once it reaches `ff` in the path.  Then, `.eonp()` will be false because the end of the `Node4`'s path has not been reached (also, `.div()` will return `true` since the act of following the given path `div`erged from all Trie paths encoded in the nodes).  `.eop()` will be `false` because we're not at the end.

Suppose we instead walk the path `00 01 02 03 04 05 06 07 ff 09`.  We'll visit the `Node256` as before, but on the second call to `.walk()`, we'll reach the end of the `Node4`'s path segment and return `None` since it has no child pointer for `ff`.  Here, `.eonp()` will be `true` and `.eop()` will be false.

If we walk the path `00 01 02 03 04 05 06 07 08 ff`, we'll visit the `Node256` on the first call to `.walk()`, the `Node4` on the second call to `.walk()`, and the `Leaf` on the third call to `.walk()`.  Here, `.eonp()` will be `false` (since we did not consume the `Leaf`'s path segment fully) and `.eop()` will be `false`.

Only if we walk the path `00 01 02 03 04 05 06 07 08 09` will `.eop()` be true on the third call to `.walk()`.  After the first two calls to `.walk()`, we'll get back a pointer to another node and `.eonp()` will be `true`.

Underneath, we try and perform uploads in parallel with each thread using the `buffer-size` that was set (code [permalink](https://github.com/dnanexus/dx-toolkit/blob/a86d952d5142dc01a18d64f50d5049a0f7e39dbe/src/python/dxpy/bindings/dxfile.py#L513)). We submit these to task to worker pools. Which AFAIK is unaware of the current memory on the worker. What can happen is:

```
num_active_threads * buffer-size > memory availble on machine
```

While your change is less prone failing to upload in `partLimits` I think you're more prone to making the worker going OOM.

My initial idea for a fix is:
1. Make the initial `buffer-size` calculation _slightly_ worker aware (i.e never choose a buffer size greater than the memory on the worker).
2. Modify writes to be aware of the pool and current memory on the machine.

```

Thinking about this a bit more, this logic seems incorrect?

- `aliased_group_bundle_permission` is all the group bundle permissions for the object
- `aliased_cl_user_group` is all the group memberships for the user
- `aliased_cl_user_group.c.user_id == user_id` doesn't do anything because this is already enforced by the join.
- So it seems like if the bundle grants access to some group A, and the user X belongs to some group B, then user X incorrectly gets access to the bundle?
- Seems like what we're missing here is we need to check that `aliased_group_bundle_permission.c.group_id == aliased_cl_user_group.c.group_id`?
- Alternatively you can push this constraint into the join condition i.e. when you in onto `aliased_group_bundle_permission`, join using the condition that the bundle permission group is the user's groups or the public group only?

I see required options as just one special case of option validation - and in our case not even a common one. Other kinds of option validation include "if this option is specified then this other one must not be"  or "if this option has this value then this other option must be present", and so on.   And since all those must be enforced manually, I'm not sure it's worth carving out a special affordance for that one special case?

Since nobody ever sends a `JobAnnotationMessage` anymore, and instead we always send `BatchSystemMessage` to communicate e.g. the AWS Batch job ID, then the annotations here will always be empty.

1. If they are always empty, it might make sense to remove the field, and the `JobAnnotationMessage` altogether.
2. Whether you do that or not, properly supporting AGC's dialect of WES requires always including the AWS Batch job ID in the task names that the server reports. Right now we do that by [selecting a filter function](https://github.com/DataBiosphere/toil/blob/88d4589592eba433d10b3c835ef2a0be5653336a/src/toil/server/wes/toil_backend.py#L602-L611) that gets to see the annotations and modify the WES tasks, and then [applying the filter function](https://github.com/DataBiosphere/toil/pull/4292/files#diff-e05a9b10abd96d5732ff2a3577a21843f0c6efa1074f2e44d752670449124edbR238-R249) when we go to generate the tasks. But if we no longer keep the AWS Batch job ID in the annotations, the current filter function for AWS Batch won't work, and we will break AWS Batch support.

We really ought to have the AWS WES dialect under test if we want to not break it. But, failing that, we definitely need to adapt the filter function setup to be able to put the new `external_batch_id` field from the `JobStatus` into the task name, instead of looking at the annotations. Maybe we need to give the filter_function access to the whole `JobStatus` and not just the annotations field?

The user expects the number of parallel mapper tasks to be what they give for read `parallelism`, but if `parallelism` is set too high, this can result in blocks that are much smaller than `batch_size`, and since we bundle blocks to try to achieve a full `batch_size` batch in our map tasks, the map parallelism can unexpectedly be a lot smaller than the read `parallelism`, which is what we're trying to call out here. All that decreasing `parallelism` would do in this case is result make the read-time parallelism more closely match the transformation-time parallelism.

ContextVars are just the 21st century way to do Thread-Local Variables, so yeah, now that we've got the module, CURRENT_TASK should definitely be updated (or replaced).

IMO, RUNTIME should probably not be global at all, which is why I didn't just add this flag to it. Assuming that there is only one RUNTIME could cause all sorts of scalability problems in future versions of Pipeline API.

Thankfully, RUNTIME seems to be used only in the one file, so it isn't "really" used as a Global yet and we could get rid of it before someone uses it that way.

IMO, ContextVars are actually only like 10% as bad as "true" globals. The one thing you do not want is that when you refactor your app to be multi-threaded or multi-runtimed that you'll get leaking between the threads or runtimes and ContextVars *should* do a good job of preventing that.

the ufo2ft integration tests only check the TTX dump of the TTFont that comes out ufo2ft's own compile functions. Usually that's going to be saved (compiled) to disk. The compile operation will also take care dropping the glyph names if post was set to format 3.0 (the tables which had been previously decompiled will be recompiled using the old names, post 3.0 will no longer contain the glyph names, such that upon reopening the same font the glyph names will be made up by fonttools from cmap or AGL names).

In general the problem when renaming or dropping glyph names is that there may already be tables that had been loaded using the old glyph names, and thus need to be compiled using the old names. Which is why renaming has to happen as first thing before any other table has been loaded, or else one needs to first reload the font (i.e compile all tables that had been loaded using old names, or simply copy the ones that haven't been loaded and are still binary blobs in the SFNTReader), and then they can change the names on the new TTFont instance.

It would be best to make BaseFormHandler a subclass of abc.ABC, and mark the abstract methods using the abstractmethod decorator. It looks like that we process all `OldViewPrePrepareReply` (from all nodes) regardless if the PrePrepare has been already ordered. That means that
1) PREPARE will be sent multiple times (that's why we see `PREPARE request already received` sometimes). Doesn't break anything, but just inconsistent a bit.
2) `_reordered_in_new_view` is called on every received `OldViewPrePrepareReply` which means that 
- we call `self._stasher.process_all_stashed(STASH_VIEW_3PC)` which may unstash more than needed if there are multiple view changes in a row. Should not break anything, but just inconsistent a bit.
- we send `ReOrderedInNewView` multiple times, which resets monitor.  Again, should not break anything, but just inconsistent a bit. A risk of false positive view changes.

This ends up silently losing rsc parallelism for all the `zinc-only` scala targets (which is going to be the vast majority of targets), which is why I was thinking splitting the enum up into `-java` and `-scala` versions was a good idea. `zinc-only` targets still only depend on the rsc compiles of their dependencies -- except for targets with any java sources, since javac can't yet depend on rsc output.

As per https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/7227/files#diff-074dd871ff62c835ab90d4a0a1264a63R314 or https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/7227/files#diff-074dd871ff62c835ab90d4a0a1264a63R549: if we're electing to use this coarser strategy to avoid introducing the intermediate `zinc_scala_classpath_from_rsc` product in this diff, I would want an extremely clear TODO describing:
- what parallelism we're dropping (all non-rsc targets are waiting for the zinc compiles of all their dependencies).
- what changes to the dependency graph must be made in followups.

The end result of the mixed compile changes in terms of the execution graph and intermediate products was documented in several comments in #7227 and I'm concerned about losing that and mysteriously losing parallelism later if the performance implications of the dependency graph of the zinc jobs isn't documented here.

This is of course the most complex part of the whole thing and will be changing a lot, and imho this would become significantly more maintainable if we spent a small amount of time converting to v2 rules and letting the v2 engine construct the graph implicitly.

> Is there a way to do this without starting a subprocess?

@jakirkham Yes, another approach is to unset `"CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES"` in `os.environ` before calling `cuInit()` in the main process.

```python
# Empty `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` can cause `cuInit()` returns `CUDA_ERROR_NO_DEVICE`
# Invalid `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` can cause `cuInit()` returns `CUDA_ERROR_INVALID_DEVICE`
os.environ.pop("CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES", None)
```

The point here using subprocess is separate the `.so` handle (`fd`s) namespaces. Although it adds more complexity.

1. We never release the `.so` handle once we load `libcuda.so`. The `conda` program will always keep the NVIDIA device (`/dev/nvidia-uvm`) and kernel module (`nvidia_uvm`) in use while solving the environment. A benefit of using a subprocess here is we can release the `fd` as the subprocess ends. We use caches to memorize the result, so the subprocess will start only once.
2. Currently, `conda` is a standalone program, and it is not meant to be `import`ed. Unset and `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` and call `cuInit()` in the main process seems safe here. But if someone calls `cuInit()` with invalid `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` in advance. Then unsetting `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` and call `cuInit()` in conda internals will always fail. The `.so` handlers and `fd`s are messed up in the main process.

That only works if `_from_generated(None)` returns `None`. But based on the examples in the `Tables` SDK, the `_from_generated` pattern says that `_from_generated(None)` should return an instance of the referenced type, not `None`.

`Monitor`, and `Storage` has the same pattern as `Tables`.

Several other SDKs don't have a special case for an empty `generated`.

Having said that, the `AppConfiguration` and the `Search` SDKs return None if `generated` is None, an instance of the class otherwise.

So the treatment of `generated` is inconsistent within the various Azure SDKs. The overall consensus is that a `._from_generated` method should return an instance of its class, but there are at least two SDKs that use a different pattern that returns `None` if the `generated` is `None`.

`remoteSessionRef` is really our session ID. From [`packages/core/plan.md`](https://github.com/nteract/nteract/blob/df958a94565520df1645b54ba7218b24be4e1a6a/packages/core/plan.md#core-concepts):

- `ref` - an internal _reference_ to an entity upon _recognition_, e.g. kernels, hosts, kernelspec collections, etc.
- `id` - likely an external identifier, e.g. with /api/kernels/9092, 9092 is the id

> We use the term _recognition_ over _creation_ because we want to have a way to
reference an entity _before_ we get a response from some api. A good example is
having a _ref_ for an active kernel before the kernel has been launched with a
jupyter notebook server. Since there will be a proliferation of id-strings, the
internal ones are called \_ref_s and they are only meant for use inside the
application--i.e., they have no meaning externally. The external id-string that
will typically be found is called `id`.

Not sure if I understood you correctly. `Sequential` is not intended to be a replacement for `Compose`. Instead, it should be used inside `Compose` the same way `OneOf` or `OneOrOther` are used now. 

The main use case for AutoAlbument is the following: we have N sub-policies, each sub-policy contains M augmentations.
We want to choose one sub-policy at random and apply its augmentations in a sequence. With `Sequential` we can implement this pipeline. For example, here is a definition if a pipeline that contains 3 sub-policies and each subpolicy has 2 sequential augmentations:

```
transform = A.Compose([
    A.OneOf([
        A.Sequential([
            A.HorizontalFlip(p=0.5),
            A.ShiftScaleRotate(p=0.5),
        ]),
        A.Sequential([
            A.VerticalFlip(p=0.5),
            A.RandomBrightnessContrast(p=0.5),
        ]),
        A.Sequential([
            A.RGBShift(p=0.5),
            A.Cutout(p=0.5),
        ])
    ], p=1)
])
```

> As to the API, are we requiring a relationship between the generator and generated that means that this only needs to compare the name (or filename for now, I suppose)?

I think the only notion of "above" and "below" (i.e. locality) could be the `sources` field of the generated target. Generated targets have no concrete physical location, only the location of their generator + some `generator_name`. If you generate `project:lib#subdir/foo.py`, you're not actually creating a target in `project/subdir`. You're creating a target with `spec_path=project/` and `sources=["subdir/foo.py"]`. It's not possible to create a target in `project/subdir` - the `spec_path` is always the `target_generator`. So, I don't think we have to worry about generating targets above you. I think it should be that the user started the SRP client manually and then enables `auto start` mode when the SRP client is already running. 
This seems to be a mixed use of `auto start` and `manual` modes, which could make things more complex than necessary. 

I think it would be slightly better if there is a distinct boundary between the two modes:
- `auto start` mode: always use auto selected server from network data
- `manual` mode: always use the manually specified server 

That would being following restrictions to the user APIs:
- User can not enable `auto start` mode when the SRP client is already running (manually started). User must stop the manually started SRP first. 
- User can not manually start the SRP server when `auto start` mode is enabled, even when there is no SRP server TLV in the network data (i.e. SRP client is not running). If I understand correctly, this is allowed by this PR which I think is quite a confusing behavior.

```python
def pretty_print(func):

    inner.__repr__ = inner.__str__

Re: using the methods instead of passing to the database, the main difference in behavior is that the `commit` method will [clear loaded database state](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/13/orm/session_api.html#sqlalchemy.orm.session.Session.commit), so the next execution will load the database anew. The `rollback` method has no differences in the [documentation](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/13/orm/session_api.html#sqlalchemy.orm.session.Session.rollback) compared to just executing the statement.

I'm not sure adding several conditions (if command is begin, if command is rollback, if command is commit) is worth any perceived stylistic benefit of using the methods here. So I think -- since only `commit` has any real behavioral difference compared to just executing -- it would make sense to not execute any `COMMIT` statements passed by the user. This also resolves your sub-question here, since only the `commit()` execution would happen. It would also make sense to not execute `BEGIN`, since the transaction will be started automatically by SQLAlchemy. In contrast, `ROLLBACK` can take many forms, and there's seemingly no real functionality difference, so it probably makes sense to just execute the user's query.

Proposed solution:

```python
if command not in ["BEGIN", "COMMIT"]:
    result = self._session.execute(sqlalchemy.text(statement))```

You could link back to this PR (ideally to this comment thread, by referencing the new issue from here), and ask the maintainers there to create a separate singleton sentinel value per the BDFL's guidance in the GitHub issues I listed above.

3. "pre-allocating a 'custom scores distribution' (similar like eos_dist or pad_dist for each target prefix timestep (for cases with and without vocabulary selection). I got your idea here and I need to think more about it. It is true that sum with np.inf is used with `pad_dist` and `pt.where()`. Meanwhile, my thought at the moment is also that I don't really know the benefit of adding sum instead of doing in-place `masked_fill_`. Doing masked_fill is intuitive, and it is an in-place operation so it is fast and I notice people do very often in Pytorch. Another thought is how to cover all cases of arbitrary length of prefix with this. Short version: Do you think there's any value in keeping the `self._read_pv = None` in the new `finalize` for potential side-effects? (and same for the write version of course)

Long version:

This follows suit with the example in the weakref docs, removing the `not None` check and `= None` on `_read_pv`. Same with the write. The idea being that finalizers can only be invoked once, so there's no need to guard from repeated calls.

But the weakref example is very simple; their `self.name` is just a string and setting it to `None` served no other purpose than that condition. Here, `self._read_pv` is used in many other places and is a PV object. Setting it to `None` (or not) may have other consequences (including when the PV object is garbage collected).

That said, none of the tests are failing and I can't think of any real scenario where this would make a tangible difference. I think it would only come into play if `destroy` was called manually, and the reference to the `Signal` instance kept around (or even attempted to be used again, which you shouldn't do anyway). So feel free to handwave this as a non-issue.

According to [documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#vars) (including from Py2.7), it _is_ always the case:

> `vars([object])`
> Return the `__dict__` attribute for a module, class, instance, or any other object with a `__dict__` attribute.
> (...)
> A `TypeError` exception is raised if an object is specified but it doesn’t have a `__dict__` attribute (...).

This is good if we have to distinguish a missing `__dict__` from an `AttributeError` in the same code block. However, just above the `vars()` documentation is `type()` documentation, and it states:

> `class type(object)`
> (...)
> With one argument, return the type of an object. The return value is a type object and **generally** the same object as returned by `object.__class__`.

when you just use `shell: bash`, GitHub Actions actually uses `bash --noprofile --norc -e -o pipefail {0}` (where `{0}`  is a placeholder for your code from the `run:` block). This causes this command to fail if no changes are found in the docs:

```shell
num_doc_files_changed=$(
    git diff --name-only | grep --count -E "\.Rd|NAMESPACE"
)
```

If `git diff`  doesn't match anything, the full command returns a non-zero exit code.

You can try this yourself. Run the following in any git repo with no changes (i.e. `git status` says "nothing to commit, working tree clean").

```shell
git diff --name-only | grep --count -E "abc"
echo $?
```

This isn't a problem in the existing r-package-check-docs task on `master` because we run it in a script that does not have `-e` or `-o pipefail` set.


Using `bash --noprofile --norc {0}` ensures that the job only fails when we want it to (by calling `exit` explicitly).

Hopefully not. If it does, the input data is setup incorrectly. For example two images in the same sample of different shape, or an `bounding_box.spatial_size` attribute out of sync with the corresponding image.

> What can go terribly wrong if we don't raise an error?

The first problem is that we needed to decide which value we return. `sizes.pop()` is not deterministic, so that would be quite bad.

Even if we can decide on a strategy to return a value, best case scenario is that the transform that requested the size fails loudly, albeit with a less expressive error message, when trying to transform an item that doesn't match the returned value. Worst case, the transform silently passes on bad input data.

Therefore, if you really don't want two variables, let's drop `MESSAGE_ID_FQDN` instead of `MESSAGE_ID_ON_DELIVERY`.

@annatisch This is a pretty good point of view which pushes me to think harder on this.

The right thing to do here from my point after revisiting the code base is: **It should be a top-level client parameter. And to your concern, we should ensure the parameter plays well with other receive mechanisms -- write tests for them.**

I should admit  the idea of only exposing it at the iterator looks the most simplistic. However:
  - Our goal is to keep the uamqp receiver alive in ServiceBus world which I don't think iterator only would help us achieve.
    - uamqp has three receiving mechanisms in total: `pull/push/iterator`.
    - Service bus uses `pull` and `iterator`.
      - Keeping uamqp receiver alive (not shutdown after timeout) should work for both `pull` and `iterator` mode.
      - If it's iterator only, then it means pull receive would shutdown the uamqp receiver after timeout.
  - Consistency concern: The key code to impact the behavior of "shutdown after timeout"  is in `do_work()` which is the base of all receiving mechanisms.
    - Making it generator only would bring extra complexity than it looks like, in which extra variables or flow need to be introduced to guarantee the consistency of receiver status when multiple receiving mechanisms are used simultaneously.

So overall, I believe making it a top-level parameter brings us more benefits than the costs as this meets our goal in SB and keeps the receiving flow clean and consistent in all ways.

> should not go outside the original color space (RGB -> RGB), 


But then why setting it to `ColorSpace.OTHER` instead of keeping as `ColorSpace.RGB`?

It seems like there are 2 different concepts:

- The color-space of an image: RGB vs RGBA vs greyscale (... vs. YUV vs CMYK which aren't supported in torchvision)
- The range of values, and the current assumption that it is `[0, 1.0 if image.is_floating_point() else torch.iinfo(image.dtype).max]`

Conceptually these are 2 different things to me. E.g. I would argue that an RGB image in [0-1] is **still** an RGB image even after it is normalized: the range assumption may be broken, but it's still an RGB image - just in a non-standard range. Not sure I'm convincing but:

- `normalize` (out = (X - mean) / std) is just a generalization of `convertImageDtype` (out = X / 255), and `convertImageDtype` preserves the `ColorSpace` attribute.
- passing an RGB image to `normalize`  changes its range, but it doesn't convert it to a greyscale or RGBA or YUV / CMYK.

So I think there's an argument to be made to return Images instead of Tensors after `normalize`: I agree that the assumption about the range is broken, but a) this assumption isn't encoded in the ColorSpace anyway (it's encoded in the dtype) and b) returning a Tensor doesn't prevent any more issue than returning an Image.

Under the current design, commands in the `pulumi state` subdomain are only to manipulate the state. They don't remove refresh from the resolve pending path. They provide the human interface to allow refresh to fully resolve the pending operations. `pulumi state pending resolve-create` provides an interface to convert a pending create into a pending import, which can then be resolved by running `pulumi refresh`.

> Related to the above - do we really still think refresh should partially clear pending operations but not fully? It feels like we've now changed our overall approach, and should revisit that as part of a consistent approach to pending operations? Does it make sense to undo that and incorporate non-creates into the new pulumi stack pending ... commands? Either as a pending clear that covers everything as noted above, or as a separate command for non-creates?

I think of it as `pulumi refresh` updates the state to reflect reality as much as possible by looking to the providers. This is still necessary, since `pulumi state` *only* performs local and self-contained edits to the state. We need both commands since they do fundamentally different things. 

> I'm also curious - what is the guidance for automated scenarios (CI/CD, Automation API, etc?). Is it that users should just live with the warnings, until they are ready to manually run one of these commands from outside the automated system? Or is it that they should add logic to the automation to clear these out but log/record the cleared pending operations?

Teams can decide for themselves, but I would expect that the `pulumi state` subcommand are employed manually when something has gone wrong. They should not be needed in the common case.

> Perhaps we could simplify this to:
>
> - To clear out pending operations run `pulumi stack pending clear`
> - If a resources was created in your cloud provider but not recorded by Pulumi, you can bring it under management by instead running `pulumi stack pending adopt`.

We really only need pending to fix pending creates. I think `pulumi refresh` works well for other pending ops and we should not replace it with manual (even CI assisted) state manipulation. To avoid confusion, I think we need some reference to the "create" operation in `pulumi state pending clear`, hence `clear-create`.

It feels like we could make this experience even more "pleasant" by further simplifying the warning and the set of actions needed here.

In particular, the message currently suggests three different actions, with not a ton of clarity on how to decide between these:
* `pulumi refresh`
* `pulumi stack pending resolve-create`
* `pulumi stack pending clear-create`

Perhaps we could simplify this to:
* To clear out pending operations run `pulumi stack pending clear`
* If a resources was created in your cloud provider but not recorded by Pulumi, you can bring it under management by instead running `pulumi stack pending adopt`.

I think there is an argument that we only need to even suggest the first, and could just direct to docs to learn about other options.  But that may be going too far.

This would then avoid relying on `refresh` as a path to incidentally address this - since that only really made sense if it was going to fully address the problem, which we have decided we are not comfortable with.

I'm also curious - what is the guidance for automated scenarios (CI/CD, Automation API, etc?).  Is it that users should just live with the warnings, until they are ready to manually run one of these commands from outside the automated system?  Or is it that they should add logic to the automation to clear these out but log/record the cleared pending operations?

Hi Ryan

Thanks for reviewing.  In short, I changed to override `after_import_row()` not `after_import_instance()` because it is cleaner in my view.

I was originally overriding `after_import_instance()` (note this method is misnamed, I raised a separate issue for that).  The problem with overriding `after_import_instance()` is that you can check the changed values by looking at the row, but if you want extra workflow (which is my use case), then this has to execute before the base instance is persisted (or you have to add some hacky attributes or similar).

I looked at it again and thought that overriding `after_import_row()` is cleaner, because the `original` object would be available at this point, and makes it very easy for comparisons.  Also `after_import_row()` is called after the base instance is persisted.

I updated the example in the docs - you can compare `original` with `instance` - the `row_result` contains the instance.  I'll update the method docstring to make this clearer.

> Could you explain what the `astype(np_dtype[1:])` call does, and why it's needed?

`np.ndarray.astype()` converts the array to the given type. `np_dtype` is a `str` so `np_dtype[1:] == char + str(itemsize)`. The complete call converts the array from an arbitrary endianness to native one, so that it can be read by `torch.frombuffer`. 

> Also, don't we want to pass `copy=False`?

In theory, yes, but in the current implementation, no. `torch.frombuffer` will scream at you if the underlying memory is read-only and without a copy, it is. I'm looking into ways to read from the file and store the data directly in mutable memory.

You actually can index CPU, but it doesn't have the same effect as indexing GPUs. I mean that you can do operations with tensors that are stored on "different" `cpu` contexts:

```python
import mxnet as mx
mx.cpu(0) == mx.cpu(0) # outputs True
mx.cpu(0) == mx.cpu(1) # outputs False

a = mx.nd.array([1,2,3], ctx=mx.cpu(0))
b = mx.nd.array([4,5,6], ctx=mx.cpu(1))

print(a) # outputs [1. 2. 3.] <NDArray 3 @cpu(0)>
print(b) # outputs [4. 5. 6.] <NDArray 3 @cpu(1)>
# The operation works fine:
a + b # outputs [5. 7. 9.] <NDArray 3 @cpu(0)>
``` 
Notice that `a + b` store the result in `cpu(0)`. If the operation was `b + a` the result would be stored in `cpu(1)`.

I just used the same buffer size in the other stream class we had, `GzipStream`, which @nelson-liu initially added in https://github.com/codalab/codalab-worksheets/commit/df9a7fbb783f3ae4c5b63f4fff94e33e06a308c3. The tradeoffs here are:
- if BUFFER_SIZE is too small, the Python code may add too much overhead and slow down streaming a file if we're trying to stream a large amount at a time.
- if BUFFER_SIZE is too large, then if we just try to stream a small amount (like a couple of bytes), we'll add more overhead when trying to stream just a few bytes at a time.
